headline: Lost Coral Reef Found Thriving Off West Africa After 60 Years
meta_description: Scientists rediscovered a deep-water coral reef presumed dead since the 1960s teeming with life off the coast of West Africa, revealing hidden ocean ecosystems.
summary_short: A coral reef dismissed as dead 60 years ago has been rediscovered thriving with eight coral types and eight fish species off West Africa's coast.
body:
Scientists using modern sonar technology have rediscovered a deep-water coral reef off the coast of West Africa that was flagged in historical surveys from the 1960s but assumed to be dead. The research team, led by Gérard Zinzindohoué at the Institute of Halieutic and Oceanographic Research in Cotonou, Benin, found the ecosystem teeming with life—a discovery so unexpected it mixed excitement with disbelief. The footage revealed eight different types of coral and eight fish species, including golden African snappers, blackbar soldierfish, and three-banded butterflyfish, sheltering and feeding around the coral patches. The finding, described in the journal Frontiers in Marine Science, represents the first confirmed record of a living mesophotic coral ecosystem on the Gulf of Guinea continental shelf.

Mesophotic coral ecosystems exist in deeper water where light is limited, appearing as scattered patches rather than continuous barriers. The research team surveyed 7 miles of seabed using sonar, then deployed an underwater drone and National Geographic's Deep Sea Camera System to investigate the most promising areas. The work was arduous—Mr. Zinzindohoué recalls "long days at sea, technical problems, and a lot of uncertainty" before those first images appeared on screen.

What makes this discovery particularly intriguing is what it reveals about our blind spots. The researchers haven't yet confirmed whether the corals died in the 1960s and recovered, or whether they've thrived continuously—questions that could illuminate coral resilience in our changing climate. Mr. Zinzindohoué notes that if a reef could be forgotten for six decades, "how many others are still undocumented or simply forgotten?" The historical surveys suggested a continuous reef barrier extending roughly 24 miles parallel to the coast, but much remains unexplored. For now, the rediscovered reef stands as a humbling reminder that the ocean still holds secrets—and that hope for resilient ecosystems persists in the most unexpected places.
quick_facts: Deep-water reef off West Africa rediscovered after 60-year gap
Eight coral types and eight fish species documented on site
First confirmed mesophotic reef on Gulf of Guinea continental shelf
Team surveyed 7 miles of seabed using sonar technology
